questions.text: Under what condition an object will have zero displacement but non-zero distance?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: In linear motion, an object moves along a straight line. If an object has zero displacement, it means that it ends up at the same position as it started. In this case, the distance traveled by the object will also be zero, as there is no change in position. Therefore, linear motion does not fulfill the condition of having zero displacement but a non-zero distance.

The correct answer is - Circulatory motionCirculatory motion results in zero displacement but non-zero distance because the object returns to its initial position after completing a circular path.Oscillation, on the other hand, does not result in zero displacement because:- Oscillation involves back-and-forth motion between two fixed points (e.g., a pendulum or a spring)- The object does not return to its initial position after one cycle; instead, it oscillates between two points- Displacement occurs during each cycle, even if the object returns to its starting point after multiple cyclesIn oscillation, the distance traveled is non-zero, and the displacement is also non-zero (although it may be periodic).In contrast, circulatory motion involves a complete cycle around a closed path, resulting in zero displacement.So, while both circulatory motion and oscillation involve repeated motion, the key difference lies in the path and the resulting displacement.

The random motion refers to the unpredictable and irregular movement of objects. In this type of motion, an object's displacement and distance can vary widely. While an object can have zero displacement and non-zero distance during random motion, it is not a necessary condition. The object could have any combination of displacement and distance, including both zero or non-zero values.

Oscillation refers to the back-and-forth motion around a central equilibrium point. In oscillatory motion, an object typically has zero displacements when it returns to its equilibrium position. However, the distance traveled by the object will depend on the amplitude of the oscillation. If the amplitude is zero, the object remains at the equilibrium position, resulting in both zero displacement and distance. If the amplitude is non-zero, the object will have a non-zero displacement and distance.

questions.explanation: In circular motion, an object moves along a circular path. If an object completes one full revolution and returns to its starting point, it will have zero displacements since the net change in position is zero. However, the object will have traveled a non-zero distance along the circular path. The distance traveled is equal to the circumference of the circle. Therefore, circular motion satisfies the condition of having zero displacement but a non-zero distance.

We know that the object is making 14 revolutions in 25 seconds, so the time for one revolution is: t = 25 s / 14 = 1.79 s/rev The angular velocity of the object can be calculated using the formula: ω = 2π / t where t is the time for one revolution. Substituting the given values, we get: ω = 2π / 1.79 s/rev = 3.51 rad/s Now we can calculate the centripetal acceleration using the formula: a = rω2 Substituting the given values, we get: a = (0.8 m)(3.51 rad/s)2 = 9.91 m/s2 Therefore, the magnitude of the acceleration is 9.91 m/s2, directed along the radius towards the center of the circular path.

questions.text: The force which can do no work on the body on which it acts:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option suggests that the frictional force is the force that can do no work on the body on which it acts. Frictional force opposes the relative motion between two surfaces in contact. When an object moves on a surface due to an applied force, the frictional force acts opposite to the direction of motion, and it does negative work, opposing the motion. However, if the object does not move or there is no relative motion, the frictional force does no work.

This option suggests that the elastic force is the force that can do no work on the body on which it acts. Elastic force is the force exerted by a stretched or compressed elastic material, such as a spring. When a spring is stretched or compressed, it exerts an elastic force in the opposite direction, trying to return to its equilibrium position. The elastic force can do work when it is involved in stretching or compressing an elastic material, causing a displacement.

This option suggests that the gravitational force is the force that can do no work on the body on which it acts. Gravitational force is the force of attraction between two masses. When an object is at a fixed height (not moving vertically), the gravitational force does no work because there is no vertical displacement. However, when an object moves vertically, such as in free fall or when lifted against gravity, the gravitational force can do work by changing the object's potential energy.
